mappings.rationaleAI: Core developmental biology covering development scope (A), growth (B.1), morphogenesis (B.2), differentiation (B.3), control through Sonic hedgehog signaling (B.4), animal development from fertilized egg (D.1, D.2.a), organ formation (D.3), aging (E), and identical twins (F.4).
Stem cell differentiation addresses differentiation (B.3) and developmental control (B.4), while regenerative medicine relates to biological regeneration (F.1) and in vitro development (F.5).
Cell division and differentiation address growth (B.1), differentiation (B.3), and programmed cell death as developmental control (B.4).
Plant evolution addresses life cycles and alternation of generations (C.1), pollination (C.2.b), seedling development (C.3), and sporophyte development (C.4).
Gene expression through cell division relates to differentiation (B.3) and control of development (B.4).
Growth hormone addresses control of development (B.4), and hormonal maturation relates to postembryonic development (D.4).
Uncontrolled cell growth relates to biological malformation (F.3).
Gene expression mechanisms address differentiation (B.3) and molecular control of development (B.4).
Developmental biology across phyla addresses animal development (D), embryo formation (D.2), and organ formation (D.3).
Human ageing addresses senescence in mammals (E.1), causes of aging (E.2), and duration of life (E.3).
Mammalian reproductive strategies address animal development (D) and postembryonic development (D.4).
Shows how symbiotic interactions with microbiomes influence biological development and growth processes in both plants and animals (B.4).
Covers mathematical modeling of pattern formation including Turing patterns — directly relevant to morphogenesis and control of development.
Covers molecular recognition and biological processes from fertilization to organismal development — relevant to differentiation and preparatory events.
Covers puberty and brain development during transformation from childhood to adulthood — directly relevant to postembryonic development.
